edges.description: Loss of LIAS-dependent lipoylation impairs pyruvate, alpha-ketoglutarate, and branched-chain ketoacid dehydrogenase complexes.
edges.source: Defective lipoic acid synthesis via LIAS deficiency

edges.description: LIAS loss reduces lipoate-dependent glycine cleavage activity, producing the variant nonketotic hyperglycinemia branch of the disease.
edges.source: Defective lipoic acid synthesis via LIAS deficiency

edges.description: Reduced pyruvate dehydrogenase flux causes pyruvate accumulation and lactate production.
Mitochondrial energy failure and muscle weakness contribute to neonatal hypotonia.
Impaired TCA-cycle energy metabolism is associated with severe respiratory deficiency in lipoate-assembly disorders.
Severe respiratory deficiency, extreme muscle weakness, and impaired energy metabolism contribute to poor growth and failure to thrive in severe neonatal LIAS disease.
Reduced glycine cleavage causes systemic glycine accumulation.
Glycine cleavage failure can raise brain glycine and drive neurologic toxicity.
Brain glycine accumulation and encephalopathy are associated with neonatal-onset epilepsy in lipoate-assembly disorders.
Encephalopathy and neurodegeneration impair early neurodevelopment.
Elevated brain glycine can drive neurodegeneration and encephalopathy; reported LIAS cases include cortical involvement, providing the structural CNS branch for cerebral atrophy.
Reduced lipoamide on mitochondrial proteins reports the proximal LIAS-dependent protein-lipoylation defect.
edges.source: Defective lipoic acid synthesis via LIAS deficiency

edges.description: Reduced pyruvate dehydrogenase activity reports impaired lipoate-dependent alpha-ketoacid dehydrogenase function.
Higher glycine reports impaired lipoate-dependent glycine cleavage activity.
Higher lactate reports pyruvate diversion caused by impaired lipoate-dependent PDH activity.

edges.target: Defective lipoic acid synthesis via LIAS deficiency

nodes.description: Elevated brain glycine from impaired glycine cleavage contributes to the neurologic branch of LIAS deficiency, including neonatal-onset epilepsy, encephalopathy, and neurodegeneration.

evidence.reference_title: Progress in the Enzymology of the Mitochondrial Diseases of Lipoic Acid Requiring Enzymes.
evidence.snippet: Lack of glycine cleavage activity additionally results in elevated brain glycine levels which can result in a host of neurological disorders, including neurodegeneration, encephalopathy, and neonatal-onset epilepsy

evidence.reference_title: Variant non ketotic hyperglycinemia is caused by mutations in LIAS, BOLA3 and the novel gene GLRX5.
evidence.snippet: Patients with lipoate synthase-deficient variant nonketotic hyperglycinemia varied in severity from mild static encephalopathy to Leigh disease and cortical involvement.

evidence.reference_title: Variant non ketotic hyperglycinemia is caused by mutations in LIAS, BOLA3 and the novel gene GLRX5.
evidence.snippet: Patients with lipoate synthase-deficient variant nonketotic hyperglycinemia varied in severity from mild static encephalopathy to Leigh disease and cortical involvement.

nodes.description: LIAS deficiency reduces lipoylation of alpha-ketoacid dehydrogenase complexes, including pyruvate dehydrogenase, alpha-ketoglutarate dehydrogenase, and branched-chain ketoacid dehydrogenase. Impaired PDHc diverts pyruvate toward lactate, while impaired TCA-cycle entry and flux produce mitochondrial energy failure in high-demand tissues.

evidence.reference_title: Lipoic acid synthetase deficiency causes neonatal-onset epilepsy, defective mitochondrial energy metabolism, and glycine elevation.
evidence.snippet: Investigation of the mitochondrial energy metabolism showed reduced oxidation of pyruvate and decreased pyruvate dehydrogenase complex activity.

evidence.reference_title: Progress in the Enzymology of the Mitochondrial Diseases of Lipoic Acid Requiring Enzymes.
evidence.snippet: Patients that display absent or low lipoylation of all three enzymes have mutations in either of two genes LIAS or LIPT2

evidence.reference_title: Progress in the Enzymology of the Mitochondrial Diseases of Lipoic Acid Requiring Enzymes.
evidence.snippet: The loss of these two dehydrogenases short-circuits the citric acid cycle, resulting in severe respiratory deficiency and extreme muscle weakness

nodes.description: LIAS encodes lipoic acid synthetase, a mitochondrial iron-sulfur cluster enzyme that inserts two sulfur atoms into octanoyl-GCSH to generate lipoyl-GCSH. This is the second step in the lipoylation pathway, after LIPT2-mediated octanoyl transfer and before LIPT1-mediated lipoyl relay to the E2 subunits of alpha-ketoacid dehydrogenases. LIAS deficiency, like LIPT2 deficiency, abolishes lipoylation of all downstream targets including the glycine cleavage system. This disrupts pyruvate dehydrogenase, alpha-ketoglutarate dehydrogenase, branched-chain ketoacid dehydrogenase, and glycine cleavage, causing combined lactic acidosis and hyperglycinemia.

nodes.description: LIAS deficiency also reduces lipoylation of the glycine cleavage system H protein, impairing glycine cleavage activity. This produces elevated serum and cerebrospinal-fluid glycine and separates LIAS deficiency from LIPT1 deficiency, where GCSH lipoylation is spared.

evidence.snippet: Normal glycine levels demonstrate that the glycine cleavage system is functional whereas abnormally high glycine levels indicate that glycine cleavage is defective.

nodes.description: Elevated glycine in plasma and urine due to impaired glycine cleavage system lipoylation. A distinguishing feature shared with LIPT2 deficiency but absent in LIPT1 deficiency.
